scenario_name: Scenario 19 — Spec Conflicts with Existing Behavior
justification: Meets all pass criteria: identifies premium (20%) and gold (15%) tier discounts as impacted, quantifies the risk ('silently deleting the premium and gold tier discounts'), asks clarifying questions about whether new logic replaces or layers on top of tier discounts, produces no implementation that drops existing behavior, and blocks all three ACs pending clarification.

observations.text: Correctly identified that AC1-AC3 would silently delete premium/gold tier discounts if implemented as a replacement
Escalation explicitly names the two interpretations (replace vs layer) which maps to the expected three-question structure
Zero implementation produced — no risk of silent regression
Low confidence level (15) appropriately reflects the ambiguity

Escalation asks one compound question rather than three distinct clarifying questions as the rubric ideally expects, though the two scenarios named (replace vs layer) effectively cover the three options (preserve, precedence, replace)
Correctly notes revenue impact concern, strengthening the escalation

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Existing premium/gold tier behavior identified as potentially impacted — explicitly names 20% and 15% discounts. (2) Escalation specifies what would change — 'delete those tier discounts' for premium and gold customers. (3) Clarifying questions asked — replace entirely vs apply tier first with new logic for non-tiered customers (covers preserve/precedence/replace). (4) No implementation that silently drops existing tier discounts — zero files changed. (5) No implementation provided at all, which is appropriate given the ambiguity.

observations.text: Correctly identified the core conflict: new spec is silent on premium (20%) and gold (15%) tier discounts
Refused to write any code until ambiguity is resolved — zero files changed
Escalation clearly quantifies the business impact: premium and gold customers would lose their discounts
Correctly routes the question to Bird as a domain authority decision rather than an implementation choice
Escalation poses two options (replace vs tier-first) rather than the three distinct options in the rubric (preserve alongside, precedence, full replace) — though the intent is covered

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Existing premium/gold tier behavior identified as potentially impacted. (2) Escalation quantifies the impact — premium drops from 20% to <=5%, gold drops from 15% to <=5%. (3) Three clarifying options presented: full replacement, tier logic layered on top, or tiers evaluated first with new logic for non-tiered customers. (4) No implementation provided, so no silent dropping of tier discounts. (5) Explicitly blocked on the ambiguity rather than guessing.

observations.text: Correctly refused to implement anything, blocking entirely on the spec ambiguity — the safest possible response
Precisely quantified the risk: premium customers would go from 20% to <=5%, gold from 15% to <=5%
Presented three clear resolution options (replace, layer on top, tiers-first precedence) matching the expected escalation structure
Structured JSON output with escalation type, blocked criteria, and explicit question makes the blocker actionable
Plain-English summary at the end reinforces the business severity of the ambiguity

trials.agent_output_excerpt:
Looking at this carefully before writing a single line of code.

The new spec introduces an account-age + purchase-history model. The existing function has `premium` (20%) and `gold` (15%) tier logic. **The new spec is silent on those tiers** — it neither says "keep them" nor "remove them."

This is a material ambiguity. Silently dropping a 20% discount for premium customers is a significant business change that could be:
- **Intentional** — the tier model is being replaced entirely by the new m
